title: Write a basic anonymous block with BEGIN, DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE, END;, and /.

points: BEGIN starts the executable section.
END; closes the PL/SQL block.
/ executes the block in SQL*Plus.
Every PL/SQL instruction ends with ;.
DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(...) prints output when SET SERVEROUTPUT ON; is enabled.

practice.label: Print Hello World
Print your name
Print a learning goal

drill: Write an anonymous block from memory that prints 'I am learning PL/SQL' using DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE.

title: Declare a CONSTANT value.

points: A constant is declared with CONSTANT.
A constant must receive a value when declared.
A constant cannot be changed later in BEGIN.
Use constants for fixed values such as discount rate, tax rate, maximum salary, or limits.

practice.label: Use CONSTANT for tax rate
Use CONSTANT for max attempts
CONSTANT with VARCHAR2

drill: Write a block from memory that declares a CONSTANT for a fixed bonus amount and uses it in a calculation.

title: Create and use a custom RECORD type.

points: A RECORD is a user-defined composite type.
It is declared in the DECLARE section with TYPE ... IS RECORD.
After declaring the record type, declare a variable of that type.
Access fields with record_variable.field_name.
%ROWTYPE copies all table columns; RECORD lets you choose only needed fields.

practice.label: Create a RECORD with first_name, salary, hire_date
Create a RECORD for department columns
RECORD with 4 fields from employees

drill: Write a block from memory that declares a custom RECORD type with first_name, last_name, and salary, fetches employee 100, and prints the data.

title: Practice ELSIF.

points: PL/SQL uses ELSIF, not ELSEIF.
ELSIF lets you test more than two cases.
Conditions are checked from top to bottom.
The first true branch runs; the remaining branches are skipped.
ELSE is optional and handles the final default case.

practice.label: Three salary ranges: High, Medium, Low
Letter grade: A, B, C, Fail
Age categories

drill: Write a block from memory that uses ELSIF to print 'Cold', 'Mild', or 'Hot' based on a temperature value.

title: Practice FOR i IN 1..10 LOOP.

points: FOR loop implicitly declares the loop counter as an integer.
The loop counter is automatically incremented each iteration.
You cannot modify the loop counter inside the loop body.
The bounds can be numeric literals or variables.
No need to DECLARE the loop counter — it is created automatically.
Close the loop with END LOOP;.

practice.label: Print numbers 1 to 5
Print 'Iteration X' for 1..3
Print multiples of 2 (using step logic)

drill: Write a FOR loop from memory that prints numbers from 1 to 10.

title: Practice FOR i IN REVERSE 1..10 LOOP.

points: REVERSE makes the loop count down from the upper bound to the lower bound.
The range is still written as lower..upper, not upper..lower.
REVERSE 1..10 produces 10, 9, 8, ..., 1.
The loop counter still cannot be modified inside the loop.
Close with END LOOP;.

practice.label: Countdown from 10 to 1
Print 'T-minus X' from 5 down to 1
Print even numbers from 10 down to 2

drill: Write a FOR REVERSE loop from memory that prints numbers from 10 down to 1.

title: Write a full block with DECLARE, variables, BEGIN, and END;.

points: DECLARE is used before BEGIN to declare variables, constants, and types.
DECLARE is optional, but BEGIN and END; are mandatory.
Variable assignment uses :=.
String concatenation uses ||.
Variables declared in DECLARE can be used in the executable section.

practice.label: Declare v_city and print it
Declare v_age and print with label
Declare two variables and concatenate

drill: Write a full PL/SQL block from memory that declares a variable v_score, assigns it 95, and prints 'Score: 95'.

title: Practice IF THEN.

points: IF THEN executes instructions only when the condition is true.
If the condition is false and there is no ELSE, nothing runs.
A PL/SQL condition ends with END IF;.

practice.label: Test IF with v_salary = 5000 (false, prints nothing)
Test IF with v_age >= 18
Test IF with a string comparison

drill: Write a block from memory that checks if a number is positive and prints 'Positive' if true.

title: Practice IF THEN ELSE.

points: IF THEN ELSE chooses between two branches.
The THEN branch runs when the condition is true.
The ELSE branch runs when the condition is false.
Exactly one branch runs.
Close the structure with END IF;.

practice.label: Test with v_salary = 3000 (else branch)
Check if a number is even or odd
Use with BOOLEAN variable

drill: Write a block from memory that checks if a number is greater than or equal to 10 and prints 'Pass' or 'Fail'.

title: Declare variables using := and DEFAULT.

points: Variables can be initialized in the DECLARE section.
:= assigns an initial value.
DEFAULT also assigns an initial value.
Variables initialized in DECLARE can still be changed later in BEGIN.
Runtime assignment also uses :=.

practice.label: Use only := for two variables
Use only DEFAULT for two variables
Mix := and DEFAULT, reassign in BEGIN

drill: Write a block from memory that declares one variable with := and another with DEFAULT, then prints both values.

title: Practice LOOP ... EXIT WHEN.

points: Basic LOOP has no condition at the beginning.
EXIT WHEN condition; stops the loop when the condition becomes true.
If the exit condition never becomes true, the loop is infinite.
Update the loop variable inside the loop.
Close with END LOOP;.

practice.label: Print 1 to 3 with EXIT WHEN
Exit when counter reaches 7
Double the counter, exit when > 100

drill: Write a block from memory that uses LOOP with EXIT WHEN to print numbers 1 through 5.

title: Explain variable scope in nested PL/SQL blocks.

points: A PL/SQL block can contain another block.
Inner blocks can access variables from outer blocks.
Outer blocks cannot access variables declared inside inner blocks.
If an inner variable has the same name as an outer variable, the inner one hides the outer one inside that inner block.

practice.label: Nest one block inside another, different names
Three levels of nesting
Inner block uses outer variable (no shadowing)

drill: Write a block from memory with two levels of nesting where the inner block shadows an outer variable.

title: Intentionally trigger NO_DATA_FOUND with SELECT ... INTO.

points: NO_DATA_FOUND is raised when SELECT ... INTO returns zero rows.
It is a predefined Oracle exception (ORA-01403).
Handle it in an EXCEPTION block with WHEN NO_DATA_FOUND THEN.
Without exception handling, the block will terminate with an error.
Always consider NO_DATA_FOUND when using SELECT ... INTO.

practice.label: Try employee_id 9999 (non-existent)
Handle NO_DATA_FOUND with friendly message
WHERE clause that matches no rows

drill: Write a block from memory that tries to SELECT ... INTO with a non-existent employee ID and handles the NO_DATA_FOUND exception.

title: Use NOT NULL with initialization.

points: A NOT NULL variable cannot contain NULL.
A NOT NULL variable must be initialized in the declaration.
Initialization can use := or DEFAULT.
Assigning NULL later to a NOT NULL variable raises an error.

practice.label: NOT NULL with := initialization
NOT NULL with DEFAULT initialization
Multiple NOT NULL variables

drill: Write a block from memory that declares two NOT NULL variables, one with := and one with DEFAULT, and prints both.

title: Use %ROWTYPE to store one full employee row.

points: %ROWTYPE gives a variable the structure of a full table row.
Pattern: variable_name table_name%ROWTYPE;
Use SELECT * INTO row_variable when selecting a complete row.
Access column values with row_variable.column_name.
%ROWTYPE is useful when you need many columns from the same row.

practice.label: Fetch employee 101 using %ROWTYPE
Fetch employee 200 and print hire_date
Print all key columns of employee 100

drill: Write a block from memory that uses %ROWTYPE to fetch employee 100 and print their full name and salary.

title: Use %TYPE with columns from employees or departments.

points: %TYPE gives a PL/SQL variable the same type as a table column or another variable.
Pattern: variable_name table_name.column_name%TYPE;
Use %TYPE when selecting table column values into variables.
%TYPE avoids guessing column datatypes manually.

practice.label: Fetch job_id and salary for employee 101
Fetch hire_date for employee 200
Fetch first_name and last_name with %TYPE

drill: Write a block from memory that uses %TYPE to declare a variable matching employees.salary, fetches the salary of employee 100, and prints it.

title: Practice searched CASE.

points: Searched CASE tests full conditions, not fixed values.
Do not put a variable after CASE.
Each WHEN contains a condition.
Conditions are checked from top to bottom.
The first true condition runs.
Close with END CASE;.

practice.label: Score ranges with 4 levels
Temperature ranges
Age-based pricing

drill: Write a block from memory that uses searched CASE to print a performance level based on a score.

title: Use SELECT ... INTO to fetch exactly one employee.

points: SELECT ... INTO fetches column values into PL/SQL variables.
The query must return exactly one row.
Each selected column must have a matching INTO variable.
Use %TYPE to declare variables matching table column types.
Variables must be listed in the same order as the columns in SELECT.

practice.label: Fetch employee 101 with name and email
Fetch salary and job of employee 200
Fetch hire_date of employee 100

drill: Write a SELECT ... INTO block from memory that fetches the first_name, last_name, and salary of employee 100 from the employees table.

title: Use SELECT ... INTO with an aggregate such as AVG(salary).

points: Aggregate functions like AVG, SUM, MAX, MIN always return exactly one row.
Use SELECT ... INTO with aggregates to store computed values in variables.
Variables receiving aggregate results should be declared with sufficient precision.
AVG returns a NUMBER that may have decimal places.
Use ROUND to format numeric output cleanly.

practice.label: Find the maximum salary
Total salaries and employee count
Average salary in department 60

drill: Write a block from memory that calculates and prints the average salary of all employees.

title: Use SELECT * INTO v_emp with %ROWTYPE.

points: SELECT * INTO a %ROWTYPE variable fetches all columns from one row.
The %ROWTYPE variable must match the table from which you select.
Access individual columns with variable.column_name.
SELECT * INTO must return exactly one row.
Use TO_CHAR to format DATE columns when printing.

practice.label: Fetch employee 101 with %ROWTYPE
Fetch employee 200 and print hire_date
Use a variable for employee_id in WHERE

drill: Write a block from memory that uses SELECT * INTO with %ROWTYPE to fetch and print employee 100's full name and salary.

title: Practice simple CASE expression.

points: Simple CASE compares one expression or variable against fixed values.
Use WHEN value THEN for each possible value.
Use ELSE for the default case.
Close with END CASE;, not END IF;.
Simple CASE is useful when testing exact values like grade, status, day number, or code.

practice.label: Day of week number to name
Status codes with meanings
Grade A to F with descriptions

drill: Write a block from memory that uses simple CASE to map a day number (1-7) to its name.

title: Intentionally trigger TOO_MANY_ROWS with SELECT ... INTO.

points: TOO_MANY_ROWS is raised when SELECT ... INTO returns more than one row.
It is a predefined Oracle exception (ORA-01422).
Handle it in an EXCEPTION block with WHEN TOO_MANY_ROWS THEN.
SELECT ... INTO is designed for single-row queries only.
Use cursors or BULK COLLECT for multi-row queries.

practice.label: Try SELECT without WHERE
Handle TOO_MANY_ROWS gracefully
Try fetching all departments

drill: Write a block from memory that intentionally triggers TOO_MANY_ROWS by selecting from employees without a WHERE clause, and handles the exception.

title: Declare and print NUMBER, VARCHAR2, DATE, and BOOLEAN variables.

points: NUMBER stores numeric values.
VARCHAR2(size) stores text.
DATE stores dates; use SYSDATE for current date.
BOOLEAN can be TRUE, FALSE, or NULL.
BOOLEAN cannot be printed directly with DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE; test it with IF.
Use TO_CHAR(date_value, 'DD/MM/YYYY') to print a date clearly.

practice.label: Print only NUMBER and DATE
Add a second VARCHAR2 and print both
Test BOOLEAN with FALSE

drill: Write a block from memory that declares a NUMBER, a VARCHAR2, a DATE, and a BOOLEAN, then prints them all.

title: Practice WHILE LOOP.

points: WHILE repeats while the condition is true.
The condition is checked before each iteration.
Update the loop variable inside the loop to avoid an infinite loop.
Close the loop with END LOOP;.

practice.label: Count 1 to 10
Print squares: 1, 4, 9, 16, 25
Sum numbers 1 to 10

drill: Write a block from memory that uses a WHILE loop to print numbers 1 through 7.

title: Close a cursor with CLOSE cur_name;

memorize.syntax: CLOSE cursor_name;
points: Always CLOSE a cursor when done to release resources.
After CLOSE, %ISOPEN returns FALSE.
Closing an already closed cursor raises an error.
Cursor FOR loops automatically close the cursor, you do not need CLOSE.
Reopening a cursor requires first closing it or ensuring it is not open.

practice.label: Open and close a cursor on departments

drill: Write a block from memory that opens any cursor, displays a message confirming it is open using %ISOPEN, closes it, and displays a message confirming it is closed.

title: Declare a cursor with CURSOR cur_name IS SELECT ...

memorize.syntax:
CURSOR cursor_name IS
  SELECT column1, column2
  FROM table_name
  WHERE condition;
points: Declare cursors in the DECLARE section before BEGIN.
The cursor SELECT can include WHERE, ORDER BY, JOINs, and functions.
The cursor name follows the same naming rules as variables.
No data is fetched yet, the cursor is just defined at declaration time.
Fetch variables should be declared alongside the cursor using %TYPE.

practice.label: Declare a cursor for the departments table
Declare a cursor with an ORDER BY clause

drill: Write a PL/SQL block from memory that declares a cursor selecting first_name, last_name, and hire_date for all employees hired after January 1, 2008. Then open, fetch the first row, and display it.

title: Fetch one row into variables with FETCH cur_name INTO ...;

memorize.syntax: FETCH cursor_name INTO variable1, variable2;
points: Each FETCH retrieves the next row and advances the cursor pointer.
The number and order of variables must match the SELECT column list.
Use %TYPE to declare fetch variables that match table column types.
After the last row, FETCH does nothing and sets %NOTFOUND to TRUE.
You can FETCH a single row without a loop if you know only one row exists.

practice.label: Fetch the employee with the highest salary
Fetch the first two employees in department 50

drill: Write a block from memory that opens a cursor on employees ordered by hire_date DESC, fetches the most recently hired employee into variables, displays the name and hire date, then closes the cursor.

title: Rewrite a manual cursor as a cursor FOR loop

points: Cursor FOR loop automatically handles OPEN, FETCH, EXIT WHEN %NOTFOUND, and CLOSE.
No need to declare the loop variable; it is implicitly declared as cursor%ROWTYPE.
You cannot manually OPEN, FETCH, or CLOSE inside a cursor FOR loop.
The loop variable is read-only; you cannot assign values to its fields.
Use cursor FOR loops for cleaner, less error-prone code.

practice.label: Use cursor FOR loop for department 50 employees
Use cursor FOR loop for employees with salary > 10000

drill: Write a cursor FOR loop from memory to display the names of all employees in department 90, without using OPEN, FETCH, or CLOSE.

title: Use %FOUND in a cursor loop

points: %FOUND returns TRUE if the last FETCH returned a row.
The WHILE %FOUND pattern requires a FETCH before the loop and another inside.
%FOUND is the opposite of %NOTFOUND; use whichever reads more naturally.
Before the first FETCH, %FOUND returns NULL, not FALSE.
After the last row is fetched, %FOUND returns FALSE.

practice.label: Use WHILE %FOUND for department 30
Use WHILE %FOUND for employees sorted by salary

drill: Write a cursor loop from memory that uses WHILE %FOUND as the loop condition to display all employees in department 40.

title: Write the full explicit cursor pattern using LOOP and EXIT WHEN cur%NOTFOUND

points: The cursor must be DECLAREd before BEGIN.
Always OPEN before FETCH.
Always check %NOTFOUND after FETCH, before processing the row.
Always CLOSE after finishing the loop.
Use %TYPE for fetch variables to match table columns.

practice.label: Print employees in department 90
Print employees hired in or after 2005

drill: Write a cursor from memory that displays the first_name and hire_date of all employees in department 60.

title: Write a FOR rec IN (SELECT ...) LOOP without declaring a cursor

points: No cursor declaration needed; the SELECT is written inline inside the FOR loop.
The record variable is implicitly typed based on the query columns.
This is the shortest way to iterate over a query result.
Use when you do not need to reuse the cursor or pass parameters.
The query can include any valid SELECT: JOINs, subqueries, functions.

practice.label: FOR rec IN SELECT for department 80
FOR rec IN SELECT for departments at location 1700

drill: Write a FOR loop from memory that queries employees in department 100 directly (without declaring a cursor) and displays their first_name and job_id.

title: Use %ISOPEN before opening a cursor

memorize.syntax:
IF cursor_name%ISOPEN THEN
  -- cursor is open
ELSE
  -- cursor is closed
END IF;
points: %ISOPEN returns TRUE after OPEN and before CLOSE.
Defensive check: test %ISOPEN before OPEN to avoid 'cursor already open' error.
Test %ISOPEN before CLOSE to avoid 'cursor not open' error.
%ISOPEN is useful in complex procedures where cursor state may be uncertain.
It is a Boolean attribute that returns TRUE or FALSE.

practice.label: Check cursor state before and after OPEN
Use IF c%ISOPEN THEN CLOSE c before opening

drill: Write a block from memory that declares a cursor, checks %ISOPEN before opening, after opening, and after closing, displaying the result at each step.

title: Use %NOTFOUND in a cursor loop

points: %NOTFOUND is the most common way to exit a cursor loop.
Check %NOTFOUND immediately after FETCH to avoid processing a non-existent row.
Before the first FETCH, %NOTFOUND returns NULL.
%NOTFOUND stays TRUE after the last row; do not FETCH again without reopening.
EXIT WHEN %NOTFOUND is clearer and safer than counting rows manually.

practice.label: Use EXIT WHEN %NOTFOUND for department 80
Use EXIT WHEN %NOTFOUND for employees with commission

drill: Write a cursor loop from memory using EXIT WHEN cursor%NOTFOUND to print the names and salaries of all employees in department 100.

title: Open a cursor with OPEN cur_name;

memorize.syntax: OPEN cursor_name;
points: OPEN executes the query and positions the cursor before the first row.
Only after OPEN can you FETCH rows from the cursor.
Use %ISOPEN to confirm the cursor is ready before fetching.
Opening an already open cursor raises an error, check %ISOPEN first if unsure.
OPEN does not return any rows; you must FETCH to get data.

practice.label: Open and verify a cursor on employees in department 30
Open a cursor, fetch one row, display it

drill: Write a block from memory that declares a cursor on employees in department 90, opens it, checks %ISOPEN, fetches the first row, displays the name, and closes the cursor.

title: Rewrite a parameterized cursor using a cursor FOR loop

points: Pass parameters directly in the FOR loop: FOR rec IN cursor_name(value) LOOP.
The cursor is implicitly opened, fetched, and closed.
No explicit OPEN, FETCH, EXIT WHEN, or CLOSE needed.
You must still declare the cursor with the CURSOR statement first.
Cleaner than manual parameterized cursor; less code, fewer errors.

practice.label: Parameterized cursor FOR loop for department 60
Parameterized cursor FOR loop for department 90

drill: Write a parameterized cursor FOR loop from memory that accepts a department_id and displays the employees in that department.

title: Write a parameterized cursor with one parameter: department id

memorize.syntax:
CURSOR cursor_name(param_name datatype) IS
  SELECT ... WHERE column = param_name;
points: Declare parameters in parentheses after the cursor name, with type but no size.
Pass the value when opening: OPEN cursor_name(value).
Parameters act as placeholders in the WHERE clause.
You can use variables, literals, or expressions as argument values.
A parameterized cursor can be reopened with different values.

practice.label: Parameterized cursor for department 50
Parameterized cursor for department 90

drill: Write a parameterized cursor from memory that accepts a department_id and displays the names and salaries of all employees in that department.

title: Write a parameterized cursor with two parameters: department id and minimum salary

memorize.syntax:
CURSOR cursor_name(p1 NUMBER, p2 NUMBER) IS
  SELECT ... WHERE col1 = p1 AND col2 >= p2;
points: Separate multiple parameters with commas in both declaration and OPEN call.
Order matters; arguments are passed positionally.
Each parameter needs a datatype but no size or precision constraint.
Parameters can appear anywhere in the SELECT: WHERE, HAVING, even in subqueries.
Use descriptive parameter names to make the cursor self-documenting.

practice.label: Two parameters: department 50 and minimum salary 3000
Two parameters: department 80 and minimum salary 8000

drill: Write a two-parameter cursor from memory that accepts department_id and a minimum salary, displaying only employees who meet both criteria.

title: Reopen a parameterized cursor with different values after closing it

points: You must CLOSE the cursor before reopening it with different parameters.
Attempting to OPEN an already open cursor raises an exception.
Each OPEN re-executes the query with the new parameter values.
Cursor attributes (%FOUND, %NOTFOUND, %ROWCOUNT) reset after each OPEN.
This pattern is useful for processing the same query against different inputs.

practice.label: Reopen cursor for department 30 then department 100
Reopen cursor for department 50 then department 80

drill: Write a block from memory that opens a parameterized cursor for department 50, processes it, closes it, then reopens it for department 80.

title: Use %ROWCOUNT to print the number of fetched rows

points: %ROWCOUNT returns the number of rows fetched so far in the current OPEN.
It increments after each successful FETCH.
Before the first FETCH, %ROWCOUNT returns 0.
After the loop ends, %ROWCOUNT equals the total number of rows fetched.
Use %ROWCOUNT for row numbering, progress tracking, or limiting output.

practice.label: Number employees in department 50 with %ROWCOUNT
Number and total employees in department 60

drill: Write a cursor loop from memory that numbers each employee in department 60 as '1. First_Name', '2. First_Name', etc. using %ROWCOUNT.

title: Fetch cursor rows into a cursor %ROWTYPE record

memorize.syntax:
CURSOR cursor_name IS SELECT * FROM table_name;
rec cursor_name%ROWTYPE;
points: %ROWTYPE declares a record that matches the cursor's SELECT column list exactly.
Use cursor_name%ROWTYPE instead of table_name%ROWTYPE when the cursor does not select all columns.
Access fields with record_variable.column_name dot notation.
%ROWTYPE keeps code DRY; no need to declare individual variables.
Changes to the cursor SELECT list automatically propagate to the record structure.

practice.label: Use cursor%ROWTYPE for employees in department 90
Use cursor%ROWTYPE for departments

drill: Write a cursor from memory using cursor%ROWTYPE to display first_name and salary of all employees in department 50.

title: Explain when to use an explicit cursor: query returns multiple rows

points: Use explicit cursors when a SELECT returns more than one row (SELECT INTO would raise TOO_MANY_ROWS).
Explicit cursors let you process rows one by one in a controlled loop.
The cursor query is defined at declaration time and executed when OPENed.
Explicit cursors give access to attributes (%FOUND, %NOTFOUND, %ROWCOUNT, %ISOPEN).
For single-row queries, use SELECT INTO; for multi-row, use explicit cursors.

practice.label: Print employees in department 90
Print employees with salary above 10000

drill: Explain in your own words when to use an explicit cursor instead of SELECT INTO, then write a cursor block that displays the first_name and last_name of all employees in department 50.

title: Call a function from an anonymous block

points: Assign the function result to a variable: v_result := func_name(args);
You can also use the function call directly in DBMS_OUTPUT: DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(func_name(args));
The variable type should match the function's RETURN type.
Unlike procedures, functions can be used in expressions (e.g., IF func(x) > 10 THEN).
Always SET SERVEROUTPUT ON to see the output in SQL*Plus.

practice.label: Use function directly in output
Use function in conditional logic

drill: Write an anonymous block from memory that calls the dept_name function for department ID 30 and stores the result in a variable, then prints it.

title: Call a function from SQL using SELECT function_name(...) FROM dual;
run: SELECT dept_name(50) FROM dual;

memorize.syntax: SELECT func_name(args) FROM dual;
points: DUAL is a special one-row table in Oracle used for queries that don't need a real table.
Functions called from SQL must be stored (not local) and the user must have EXECUTE privilege.
Functions in SQL cannot contain DML (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE) or COMMIT/ROLLBACK.
Functions used in SQL must have only IN parameters (no OUT or IN OUT).
Functions returning BOOLEAN cannot be called from SQL (BOOLEAN is a PL/SQL-only type).

practice.label: Function in WHERE clause

practice.label: Function with column as argument

drill: Write a SELECT statement from memory that calls the circle_area function with radius 7 using the DUAL table.

title: Call a stored procedure from an anonymous block

points: Call a stored procedure by name followed by arguments in parentheses.
If the procedure has no parameters, use proc_name; (no parentheses needed).
You can also use named notation: proc_name(param => value).
Procedures must be called from a PL/SQL block (BEGIN...END or another procedure).
Procedures CANNOT be called directly from SQL like functions can.

practice.label: Call with positional notation
Call with named notation
Call procedure with mixed notation

drill: Write an anonymous block from memory that calls a stored procedure named log_action with a single VARCHAR2 argument 'User logged in'.

title: Create a stored function with CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION

points: Functions have a RETURN clause in the header specifying the datatype to return.
The function body must contain at least one RETURN statement.
Like procedures, functions use IS (not DECLARE) and end with END;
Functions are called differently: result := function_name(args);
Functions can also be called directly from SQL (unlike procedures).

practice.label: Function that returns full name
Function with no parameters

drill: Write a stored function get_commission from memory that takes an employee ID and returns their commission_pct (or 0 if null).

title: Create a stored procedure with CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE

memorize.syntax:
CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE name(param IN type) IS
BEGIN
  -- code
END;
/
points: CREATE OR REPLACE creates a new procedure or replaces an existing one without needing DROP first.
Stored procedures use IS, not DECLARE (DECLARE is only for anonymous blocks).
The slash (/) on its own line executes the CREATE statement in SQL*Plus.
Parameters are declared inside parentheses after the procedure name.
A procedure does not return a value with RETURN; it uses OUT parameters instead.

practice.label: Procedure that prints department name
Procedure that prints employee count

title: Use DESCRIBE on a procedure or function
run:
DESCRIBE greet_user;
DESCRIBE get_dept_name;

memorize.syntax:
DESCRIBE proc_name;
DESCRIBE func_name;
points: DESCRIBE (or DESC) shows the parameters and their modes/datatypes of a subprogram.
For procedures, it shows parameter name, mode, and datatype.
For functions, it also shows the RETURN type.
DESCRIBE works on tables, views, procedures, functions, and packages.
This is a SQL*Plus command, not SQL or PL/SQL (no semicolon needed, but it tolerates one).

practice.label: Describe a procedure with multiple parameters
practice.code: DESCRIBE get_emp_name;

practice.label: Compare DESCRIBE output for procedure vs function

title: Create a function that checks if an employee salary is greater than the department average

Compute aggregates (AVG, SUM, COUNT) and compare individual values to them.
Group by department_id when computing per-department aggregates.
Use IF/ELSE to return different values based on the comparison.
This is a common pattern: fetch individual data, fetch aggregate, compare.

practice.label: Function returning the salary difference
Function checking if salary is max in dept

drill: Write a function count_above_avg from memory that returns the number of employees in a given department who earn more than that department's average.

title: Create a function that returns a department name by department id

points: Use %TYPE to anchor parameter and variable types to table columns.
Always handle NO_DATA_FOUND when using SELECT INTO in a function.
The RETURN type should match the column type (use %TYPE for safety).
This function can be used in SELECT statements to display department names.
Wrap the SELECT INTO in the function body with the parameter in the WHERE clause.

title: Use an IN OUT parameter in a procedure

memorize.syntax: PROCEDURE name(p_param IN OUT datatype) IS
points: IN OUT parameters pass a value in AND return a (possibly modified) value back.
The caller's variable is both read and potentially overwritten by the procedure.
IN OUT must be explicitly written; the default mode is IN only.
Use IN OUT when you need to transform a value in place.
The caller must pass a variable (assignable), not a literal or expression.

practice.label: Swap two values with IN OUT
Append text with IN OUT

drill: Write a stored procedure add_tax from memory that takes an IN OUT NUMBER parameter and multiplies it by 1.19 (adds 19% tax).

title: Use an IN parameter in a procedure

memorize.syntax: PROCEDURE name(param_name IN datatype) IS
points: IN is the default parameter mode (you can omit the word IN).
IN parameters are read-only; you cannot assign a value to them inside the procedure.
IN parameters pass values from the caller to the procedure.
Use %TYPE for parameter types to anchor to a table column.
Multiple IN parameters are separated by commas.

practice.label: IN parameter with default mode omitted
Multiple IN parameters

drill: Write a stored procedure update_email from memory that takes an employee ID (IN) and a new email (IN), updates the email, and prints 'Email updated for employee X'.

title: Remember that stored procedures use IS without DECLARE

memorize.syntax:
CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE name IS
  -- variables here directly, no DECLARE
BEGIN
  -- code
END;
/
points: Anonymous blocks: DECLARE ... BEGIN ... END;
Stored procedures/functions: CREATE ... IS ... BEGIN ... END;
The IS keyword replaces DECLARE in stored subprograms.
Variables are declared directly after IS and before BEGIN.
This is a common exam trap: using DECLARE inside a stored procedure will fail.

practice.label: Fix the broken procedure
Anonymous block with DECLARE for contrast

drill: Write a stored procedure called print_date from memory that declares a DATE variable, sets it to SYSDATE, and prints it. Do NOT use the word DECLARE.

title: Declare and call a local function inside an anonymous block

points: Local functions are declared in the DECLARE section of an anonymous block.
They must include a RETURN clause and at least one RETURN statement in the body.
Like local procedures, they can access variables from the enclosing block.
Local functions cannot be called from SQL; they exist only within the PL/SQL block.
Local functions are useful for repetitive calculations within a single block.

practice.label: Local function computing a grade
Local function with table query

drill: Write an anonymous block from memory that declares a local function cube(n NUMBER) returning n*n*n, then prints cube(3) and cube(5).

title: Declare and call a local procedure inside an anonymous block

points: Local procedures are declared in the DECLARE section (before BEGIN) of an anonymous block.
They can access variables declared in the same DECLARE section (lexical scoping).
Local procedures do not need CREATE OR REPLACE; just use PROCEDURE keyword.
They exist only for the duration of the block execution.
Multiple local subprograms can be declared; order matters (forward declarations may be needed for mutual recursion).

practice.label: Local procedure with parameters
Local procedure that queries a table

drill: Write an anonymous block from memory that declares a local procedure called say_hello which prints 'Welcome to PL/SQL', then calls it.

title: Use an OUT parameter in a procedure

memorize.syntax: PROCEDURE name(p_in IN type, p_out OUT type) IS
points: OUT parameters send values back from the procedure to the caller.
Inside the procedure, OUT parameters act as uninitialized variables; you assign values to them.
The caller must provide a variable (not a literal) for each OUT parameter.
OUT parameters are useful when a procedure needs to return multiple values.
Unlike functions, procedures use OUT parameters instead of RETURN for output.

practice.label: OUT parameter returning count
Multiple OUT parameters

drill: Write a stored procedure get_salary_range from memory that takes a department ID (IN) and returns the MIN and MAX salary (both OUT) for that department.

title: Return a value from a function using RETURN value;

memorize.syntax: RETURN expression;
points: Every function must have at least one RETURN statement that sends a value back to the caller.
RETURN immediately exits the function; code after RETURN is not executed.
The returned expression must be compatible with the RETURN type declared in the header.
Multiple RETURN statements are allowed (e.g., in IF/ELSE branches).
In procedures, RETURN (without value) only exits the procedure early.

practice.label: Multiple return points in a function
RETURN with computed expression

drill: Write a function max_of_two from memory that takes two NUMBERs and RETURNs the larger one using IF/ELSE.

title: Use RETURN type in a function header

memorize.syntax: CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION name RETURN datatype IS
points: RETURN in the header declares the datatype of the value the function will return.
The RETURN type must match the actual value returned (Oracle may do implicit conversion).
Common return types: NUMBER, VARCHAR2, DATE, BOOLEAN (BOOLEAN cannot be used in SQL).
Do NOT specify size/precision in the RETURN clause (RETURN VARCHAR2, not RETURN VARCHAR2(50)).
Every execution path in the function must end with a RETURN statement.

practice.label: Function returning DATE
Function returning NUMBER with calculation

drill: Write a function get_grade from memory that takes a NUMBER score and returns VARCHAR2: 'A' if >= 90, 'B' if >= 80, 'C' otherwise.

title: Explain the difference between stored and local subprograms

memorize.syntax:
Local: declared in DECLARE, lives only in that block.
Stored: created with CREATE OR REPLACE, persists in the database.
points: Local subprograms are declared inside the DECLARE section of an anonymous block; they exist only for that session and cannot be reused elsewhere.
Stored subprograms are created with CREATE OR REPLACE and saved in the database schema permanently.
Local subprograms do not need CREATE OR REPLACE; they use the PROCEDURE/FUNCTION keyword directly in DECLARE.
Stored subprograms can be called from any session, any block, or from SQL (functions only).
Local subprograms are useful for one-off logic reuse within a single block.

practice.label: Local vs stored: compare scope
Create a stored version

drill: Write an anonymous block that contains a local procedure called print_twice which prints a given string two times. Then write the same thing as a stored procedure.

title: Query USER_OBJECTS to see created procedures/functions
run:
SELECT object_name, object_type, status, created
FROM USER_OBJECTS
WHERE object_type IN ('PROCEDURE','FUNCTION')
ORDER BY object_type, object_name;

memorize.syntax: SELECT object_name, object_type FROM USER_OBJECTS WHERE object_type IN ('PROCEDURE','FUNCTION');
points: USER_OBJECTS contains metadata about all objects owned by the current user.
Filter by object_type to find procedures and functions.
Other useful columns: status (VALID/INVALID), created, last_ddl_time.
ALL_OBJECTS shows objects the user has access to; DBA_OBJECTS shows everything (needs DBA privs).
This is the first query to run when you want to check what subprograms exist in your schema.

practice.label: Count procedures and functions
practice.code: SELECT object_type, COUNT(*) FROM USER_OBJECTS WHERE object_type IN ('PROCEDURE','FUNCTION') GROUP BY object_type;

practice.label: Check for invalid objects
practice.code: SELECT object_name, object_type, status FROM USER_OBJECTS WHERE status = 'INVALID';

practice.label: List all object types in your schema
practice.code: SELECT DISTINCT object_type FROM USER_OBJECTS ORDER BY object_type;

drill: Write a query from memory that lists all functions in your schema with their status.

title: Query USER_SOURCE to see stored source code
run:
SELECT line, text
FROM USER_SOURCE
WHERE name = 'GREET_USER'
ORDER BY line;

memorize.syntax: SELECT text FROM USER_SOURCE WHERE name = 'PROC_NAME' ORDER BY line;
points: USER_SOURCE stores the source code of your subprograms, one line per row.
The name column is the upper-cased object name (Oracle stores names in uppercase by default).
text contains the actual code; line is the line number within the source.
Use ORDER BY line to reconstruct the source in the correct order.
ALL_SOURCE and DBA_SOURCE are also available for broader views.

practice.label: Search source for a keyword
practice.code: SELECT name, line, text FROM USER_SOURCE WHERE UPPER(text) LIKE '%SELECT%' AND name = 'GET_DEPT_NAME' ORDER BY line;

practice.label: List all source objects and their line counts
practice.code: SELECT name, type, COUNT(*) AS lines FROM USER_SOURCE GROUP BY name, type ORDER BY name;

drill: Write a query from memory to see the full source code of the function IS_ABOVE_DEPT_AVG.

title: Create an AFTER INSERT trigger

memorize.syntax:
CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER name
AFTER INSERT ON table_name
BEGIN
  -- runs after the INSERT completes
END;
/
points: AFTER INSERT triggers fire after a row is inserted into the table.
The INSERT has already committed the row to the table when the trigger runs (within the same transaction).
AFTER triggers are ideal for logging/auditing because the data is already in the table.
Use BEFORE triggers when you need to validate or modify data before the insert completes.
AFTER triggers cannot change :NEW values (the insert already happened).

practice.label: AFTER INSERT that logs to a table
AFTER INSERT calling a procedure

drill: Write an AFTER INSERT trigger from memory on the departments table that prints 'Department added' using DBMS_OUTPUT.

title: Create an AFTER UPDATE trigger

memorize.syntax:
CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER name
AFTER UPDATE ON table_name
BEGIN
  -- runs after the UPDATE completes
END;
/
points: AFTER UPDATE triggers fire after rows are updated in the table.
The trigger fires even if the UPDATE statement updates zero rows (statement-level).
Use AFTER UPDATE for audit logging of changes.
Combine with IF UPDATING THEN to distinguish from other events in multi-event triggers.
AFTER UPDATE cannot prevent the update (use BEFORE for that).

practice.label: AFTER UPDATE that logs old and new values
AFTER UPDATE logging to audit table

drill: Write an AFTER UPDATE trigger from memory on the employees table that prints 'Salary column updated' but only when the salary column is updated.

title: Create a log table for trigger tests in your_schema

points: A log table is used by triggers to record audit information about DML operations.
Use GENERATED BY DEFAULT AS IDENTITY for auto-incrementing IDs (Oracle 12c+).
Include a DATE column with DEFAULT SYSDATE to timestamp each log entry.
Columns should capture: what action, on which table, old value, new value, and who.
Always create the log table BEFORE creating the triggers that write to it.

practice.label: Insert a test row manually
Query log table by action type
practice.code: SELECT action, COUNT(*) FROM trigger_log GROUP BY action ORDER BY action;

drill: Write the CREATE TABLE statement from memory for an audit_log table with columns: id (auto-increment PK), change_date (default SYSDATE), operation (VARCHAR2), and description (VARCHAR2).

title: Create a table trigger using CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER

memorize.syntax:
CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER trigger_name
BEFORE|AFTER event ON table_name
BEGIN
  -- code
END;
/
points: CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER creates a new trigger or replaces an existing one.
A trigger without FOR EACH ROW is a statement-level (table) trigger; fires once per DML statement.
The trigger body is a standard PL/SQL block (BEGIN...END).
No DECLARE section directly; declare variables between the IS/BEGIN keywords (not needed for statement triggers).
Trigger names should follow a naming convention like trg_table_event.

practice.label: Create a trigger with variable declaration
Create a BEFORE DELETE trigger

drill: Write a table-level trigger from memory called trg_dept_log that inserts a message into emp_log after any UPDATE on the departments table.

title: Memorize DELETE: :OLD has values, :NEW is null

memorize.syntax: DELETE: :OLD = deleted values, :NEW = NULL
points: On DELETE, :OLD contains all the values of the row being deleted.
On DELETE, :NEW is completely NULL because no new row is being created.
You cannot modify :NEW in a DELETE trigger (there is no new row).
:OLD can be read but not modified in any trigger.
This is the mirror of INSERT: on INSERT :OLD is NULL, on DELETE :NEW is NULL.

practice.label: Audit DELETE with :OLD values
practice.code:
CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER trg_delete_audit
BEFORE DELETE ON employees
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
  INSERT INTO trigger_log (table_name, action, old_value)
  VALUES ('employees', 'DELETE',
    :OLD.employee_id || ':' || :OLD.first_name || ' ' || :OLD.last_name);
END;
/

-- Trigger will log if any delete happens
ROLLBACK;

drill: From memory, fill in: On DELETE, :NEW is ____ and :OLD has ____.

title: Disable a trigger with ALTER TRIGGER trigger_name DISABLE

memorize.syntax: ALTER TRIGGER trigger_name DISABLE;
points: ALTER TRIGGER ... DISABLE deactivates a trigger without dropping it.
A disabled trigger still exists but does not fire on DML events.
Disabling is useful during bulk data loads to avoid trigger overhead.
Disable all triggers on a table: ALTER TABLE table_name DISABLE ALL TRIGGERS;
The trigger code is preserved; you can re-enable it later with ALTER TRIGGER ... ENABLE.

practice.label: Disable for bulk insert, then re-enable
Disable all triggers on a table

drill: Write the command from memory to disable a trigger named TRG_AUDIT_EMP.

title: Drop a trigger with DROP TRIGGER trigger_name

memorize.syntax: DROP TRIGGER trigger_name;
points: DROP TRIGGER permanently removes the trigger from the database.
Unlike tables, there is no DROP TRIGGER ... PURGE (triggers don't go to recycle bin).
You cannot rollback a DROP TRIGGER; the trigger is gone immediately.
After dropping, the trigger name disappears from USER_TRIGGERS and USER_OBJECTS.
Use DROP TRIGGER before CREATE OR REPLACE if you want a completely fresh creation (though CREATE OR REPLACE already handles this).

practice.label: Create, verify, drop, verify
Drop a trigger that no longer exists (error demo)

drill: Write the command from memory to permanently delete a trigger named TRG_OLD_AUDIT.

title: Use ELSIF DELETING THEN

points: DELETING is a boolean function returning TRUE when the trigger fired due to a DELETE.
Use ELSIF DELETING THEN as the final condition in a three-event trigger.
DELETING returns FALSE for INSERT and UPDATE events.
Statement-level DELETE triggers fire even if zero rows are deleted.
In row-level triggers, DELETE means :OLD has the deleted row values and :NEW is all NULL.

practice.label: Full three-event row trigger
DELETE prevention with DELETING

drill: Write a row trigger from memory that handles all three DML events and prints the event name plus the employee ID from :NEW (for insert/update) or :OLD (for delete).

title: Use ELSIF UPDATING THEN

points: UPDATING is a boolean function returning TRUE when the trigger fired due to an UPDATE.
Use ELSIF UPDATING THEN as the second condition in a multi-event trigger.
Note the spelling: ELSIF (not ELSEIF, not ELSE IF).
UPDATING returns FALSE for INSERT and DELETE events.
You can check if a specific column was updated with IF UPDATING('column_name') THEN.

practice.label: UPDATING specific column check
UPDATING with audit entry

drill: Write a trigger from memory that uses IF INSERTING THEN ... ELSIF UPDATING THEN ... ELSIF DELETING THEN to print the event type.

title: Enable a trigger with ALTER TRIGGER trigger_name ENABLE

memorize.syntax: ALTER TRIGGER trigger_name ENABLE;
points: ALTER TRIGGER ... ENABLE activates a trigger that was previously disabled.
Triggers are enabled by default when created with CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER.
Check trigger status with: SELECT trigger_name, status FROM USER_TRIGGERS;
You can also enable all triggers on a table: ALTER TABLE table_name ENABLE ALL TRIGGERS;
The status column shows 'ENABLED' or 'DISABLED'.

practice.label: Disable then enable and test
Enable all triggers on a table
practice.code:
ALTER TABLE employees ENABLE ALL TRIGGERS;
SELECT trigger_name, status FROM USER_TRIGGERS WHERE table_name = 'EMPLOYEES';

drill: Write the command from memory to enable a trigger named TRG_SALARY_CHECK.

title: Create a row trigger with FOR EACH ROW

memorize.syntax:
CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER name
BEFORE|AFTER event ON table_name
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
  -- :NEW and :OLD available here
END;
/
points: FOR EACH ROW is placed after ON table_name and before the BEGIN (or DECLARE) keyword.
Without FOR EACH ROW, the trigger is statement-level and runs once per DML statement.
Row triggers can reference :NEW (new row values) and :OLD (old row values).
BEFORE row triggers can modify :NEW values before they are written to the table.
AFTER row triggers cannot modify :NEW (the row is already written).

practice.label: FOR EACH ROW with DECLARE section
FOR EACH ROW with WHEN condition

drill: Write a FOR EACH ROW trigger from memory that fires BEFORE UPDATE on employees and prints the old and new salary for each updated row.

title: Use IF INSERTING THEN

points: INSERTING is a boolean function that returns TRUE when the trigger fired due to an INSERT.
Use IF INSERTING THEN inside multi-event triggers to run conditional logic.
INSERTING returns FALSE for UPDATE and DELETE events.
INSERTING can be used in both BEFORE and AFTER triggers.
This is how you make a single trigger behave differently for different DML operations.

practice.label: INSERTING with row-level trigger
INSERTING sets default values

drill: Write a multi-event trigger from memory that uses IF INSERTING THEN to set :NEW.created_date to SYSDATE on insert.

title: Insert trigger audit rows into the log table

points: Use INSERT INTO log_table inside the trigger body to record audit entries.
Capture :NEW values for INSERT, :OLD/:NEW for UPDATE, and :OLD for DELETE.
Use IF INSERTING/ELSIF UPDATING/ELSIF DELETING to build different audit messages per event.
The audit INSERT happens within the same transaction as the triggering DML.
If the DML is rolled back, the audit row is also rolled back (unless you use autonomous transaction).

practice.label: Audit trigger with user info
Minimal audit insert

drill: Write an AFTER INSERT row trigger from memory that inserts a row into trigger_log with table_name='employees', action='INSERT', and new_value = :NEW.employee_id.

title: Create a trigger for multiple events: INSERT OR UPDATE OR DELETE

memorize.syntax:
CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER name
BEFORE|AFTER INSERT OR UPDATE OR DELETE ON table_name
BEGIN
  -- fires for any of the listed events
END;
/
points: Use OR to list multiple DML events in a single trigger definition.
The trigger fires for any of the listed events (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE).
Inside the trigger, use IF INSERTING, IF UPDATING, IF DELETING to check which event fired.
This avoids creating three separate triggers for similar logic on the same table.
All listed events share the same timing (all BEFORE or all AFTER).

practice.label: AFTER multi-event with conditional logic
Multi-event that logs action type to a table

drill: Write a trigger from memory called trg_dept_monitor that fires BEFORE INSERT OR UPDATE OR DELETE on departments and prints a message for any of the three events.

title: Memorize INSERT: :NEW has values, :OLD is null

memorize.syntax: INSERT: :NEW = inserted values, :OLD = NULL
points: On INSERT, :NEW contains every column value of the row being inserted.
On INSERT, :OLD is completely NULL because there is no previous row.
You can read and modify :NEW in BEFORE INSERT triggers.
You cannot modify :OLD at all (it is read-only in all triggers).
This is a common exam question: what are :NEW and :OLD for each DML operation?

practice.label: Demonstrate :OLD IS NULL on INSERT

drill: From memory, fill in: On INSERT, :NEW has ____ and :OLD is ____.

title: Use :NEW in an INSERT or UPDATE row trigger

memorize.syntax: :NEW.column_name
points: :NEW is a pseudo-record holding the new row values during INSERT and UPDATE triggers.
In BEFORE INSERT triggers, you can assign values to :NEW.column to modify what gets inserted.
In AFTER INSERT triggers, :NEW still has values but you cannot modify them.
:NEW is always prefixed with a colon in the trigger body.
In the WHEN clause, use NEW (no colon): WHEN (NEW.salary > 10000).

practice.label: :NEW to auto-generate email
:NEW to enforce salary range

drill: Write a BEFORE INSERT row trigger from memory that sets :NEW.hire_date to SYSDATE if it is NULL, and sets :NEW.job_id to 'IT_PROG' if it is NULL.

title: Use :OLD in an UPDATE or DELETE row trigger

memorize.syntax: :OLD.column_name
points: :OLD is a pseudo-record holding the row values BEFORE the DML operation.
In UPDATE triggers, :OLD has the pre-update values and :NEW has the post-update values.
In DELETE triggers, :OLD has the row being deleted; :NEW is NULL.
:OLD is null for INSERT triggers (the row didn't exist before).
:OLD is read-only; you cannot modify :OLD values in a trigger.

practice.label: :OLD to prevent decreasing salary
:OLD in DELETE trigger to log deleted row

drill: Write a BEFORE UPDATE row trigger from memory that prevents an update if :NEW.salary is less than 50% of :OLD.salary.

title: Explain row triggers: run once per affected row

memorize.syntax:
CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER name
BEFORE|AFTER event ON table_name
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
  -- runs once per affected row
END;
/
points: A row trigger fires ONCE FOR EACH ROW affected by the DML statement.
FOR EACH ROW is the key clause that makes a trigger row-level.
Row triggers have access to :NEW and :OLD correlation values.
If the statement affects zero rows, a row trigger does NOT fire at all.
Use row triggers when you need to inspect or modify each individual row.

practice.label: Row trigger counting how many times it fires
Row trigger that modifies :NEW value

drill: Write a BEFORE INSERT row trigger from memory that automatically capitalizes the first_name of every new employee.

title: Explain table triggers: run once per statement

memorize.syntax:
CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER name
BEFORE|AFTER event ON table_name
BEGIN
  -- runs once per SQL statement
END;
/
points: A table-level (statement) trigger fires exactly ONCE per triggering SQL statement.
Even if the statement affects zero rows, the statement trigger still fires.
Table triggers do NOT have access to :NEW and :OLD (those are row-level only).
No FOR EACH ROW clause = it is a table-level trigger.
Use table triggers for operations that should happen once regardless of row count.

practice.label: Statement trigger that counts affected rows
Contrast: statement trigger firing on zero rows

drill: Write a statement-level trigger from memory that prints 'Statement completed' after any DELETE on the departments table.

title: Memorize UPDATE: :NEW has new values, :OLD has old values

memorize.syntax: UPDATE: :NEW = new values, :OLD = old values (both available)
points: On UPDATE, :NEW contains the values the row will have after the update.
On UPDATE, :OLD contains the values the row had before the update.
Both :NEW and :OLD are fully populated (not NULL) during UPDATE.
You can compare :OLD and :NEW to detect which columns actually changed.
In BEFORE UPDATE, you can still modify :NEW values before they are written.

practice.label: Detect which columns changed with :OLD/:NEW compare

drill: From memory, fill in: On UPDATE, :NEW has ____ and :OLD has ____. Can you modify :NEW in a BEFORE trigger? (Yes/No)

title: Explain what a trigger is and when it executes automatically

memorize.syntax:
CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER name
BEFORE|AFTER event ON table
BEGIN
  -- auto-executed code
END;
/
points: A trigger is a named PL/SQL block that Oracle automatically executes when a specified DML event occurs.
Triggers fire in response to INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE statements on a table (or view).
Trigger timing: BEFORE (fires before the DML) or AFTER (fires after the DML).
Triggers cannot be called directly like procedures; they fire automatically.
Triggers are used for auditing, validation, enforcing complex business rules, and maintaining derived data.

practice.label: Check trigger exists in USER_OBJECTS
practice.code: SELECT object_name, object_type, status FROM USER_OBJECTS WHERE object_type = 'TRIGGER';

practice.label: Check trigger source in USER_TRIGGERS
practice.code: SELECT trigger_name, trigger_type, triggering_event, table_name, status FROM USER_TRIGGERS;

drill: Explain in your own words: what is a trigger, and what are the three DML events it can respond to?

title: Use a trigger WHEN condition

memorize.syntax:
CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER name
BEFORE event ON table_name
FOR EACH ROW
WHEN (NEW.column condition)
BEGIN
  -- fires only when condition is true
END;
/
points: WHEN clause filters which rows trigger the row trigger; only fires if the condition is TRUE.
In the WHEN clause, use NEW and OLD WITHOUT the colon prefix.
Inside the trigger body, use :NEW and :OLD WITH the colon prefix as usual.
WHEN only works with FOR EACH ROW triggers, not statement-level triggers.
WHEN is a performance feature: the trigger body is skipped entirely when the condition is false, avoiding unnecessary PL/SQL execution.

practice.label: WHEN with OLD condition on UPDATE
WHEN on DELETE to audit only high-value deletes
WHEN with compound condition

drill: Write a row trigger from memory with WHEN (NEW.commission_pct IS NOT NULL) that prints 'Commission-based employee added' on INSERT.

title: Handle CURSOR_ALREADY_OPEN

points: CURSOR_ALREADY_OPEN is raised when trying to OPEN an already open cursor.
A cursor must be CLOSEd before it can be reopened.
Use %ISOPEN to check if a cursor is already open before calling OPEN.
This exception also fires if you try to open a cursor inside a loop without closing it.
Always CLOSE the cursor before reopening it to reset the result set.

practice.label: Check %ISOPEN before opening

drill: Write from memory an anonymous block that declares a cursor, opens it twice, and handles CURSOR_ALREADY_OPEN with proper cleanup.

title: Handle DUP_VAL_ON_INDEX

points: DUP_VAL_ON_INDEX fires when inserting a duplicate value into a unique index column.
This includes primary key columns, which have an implicit unique index.
Also raised when inserting a duplicate into a column with a UNIQUE constraint.
Always handle this exception before calling COMMIT to allow a clean ROLLBACK.
You can avoid it by checking for existence with SELECT COUNT(*) before INSERT.

practice.label: Check before insert to avoid duplicate

drill: Write from memory a block that inserts the same primary key twice into a table and catches DUP_VAL_ON_INDEX with a clear message.

title: Let an Exception Propagate to the Outer Block

memorize.syntax:
DECLARE
BEGIN
  DECLARE
  BEGIN
    -- Exception occurs here, no local handler
  END;
  -- Skipped
EXCEPTION
  WHEN exception_name THEN
    -- Catches propagating exception
END;
points: Exceptions propagate up through nested blocks until a handler is found.
The outer block can catch exceptions raised several levels deep.
Once caught, the exception stops propagating.
If no block handles it, the exception reaches the host (e.g., SQL*Plus).
Use the outermost block as a safety net with WHEN OTHERS.

practice.label: Three-level nesting with top-level handler

drill: From memory, write a two-level block where an inner block raises TOO_MANY_ROWS and the outer block catches it.

title: Add an EXCEPTION Section to an Anonymous Block

memorize.syntax:
DECLARE
  ...
BEGIN
  ...
EXCEPTION
  WHEN exception_name THEN
    -- handler code
END;
points: The EXCEPTION section is placed after the executable section and before the final END.
You can list multiple WHEN clauses for different exception types.
Each WHEN clause catches exactly one named exception.
Exceptions that are not handled will propagate outward or crash the block.
SET SERVEROUTPUT ON must be issued before running the block to see DBMS_OUTPUT.

practice.label: Add handlers to a salary query

drill: Write an anonymous block from memory with an EXCEPTION section containing two WHEN clauses: one for NO_DATA_FOUND and one for OTHERS.

title: Handle an Exception Inside the Inner Block

memorize.syntax:
BEGIN  -- outer
  BEGIN  -- inner
    -- code that raises an exception
  EXCEPTION
    WHEN exception_name THEN
      -- handle in inner block
  END;
  -- outer block continues after inner block completes
END;
points: If the inner block handles the exception, the outer block continues normally.
The exception is consumed by the inner handler.
Code after the inner block in the outer block WILL execute.
This pattern isolates risky operations in nested blocks.
Use nested blocks with their own handlers to recover from errors gracefully.

practice.label: Risky operation in inner block with recovery

drill: Write from memory a block with a nested inner block that handles NO_DATA_FOUND internally, allowing the outer block to continue normally.

title: Handle a User-Defined Exception in the EXCEPTION Section

memorize.syntax:
EXCEPTION
  WHEN user_exception THEN
    -- handle custom exception
  WHEN OTHERS THEN
    -- handle unexpected errors
END;
points: User-defined exceptions are handled exactly like predefined ones in the EXCEPTION section.
You can mix user-defined and predefined exception handlers in any order.
WHEN OTHERS still goes last, after all user-defined exception handlers.
Each user-defined exception needs its own WHEN clause.
Within the handler, you can use DBMS_OUTPUT, perform ROLLBACK, or take corrective action.

practice.label: Handle two user-defined exceptions

drill: From memory, write a block with a user-defined exception, a predefined exception handler, and WHEN OTHERS.

title: Handle INVALID_CURSOR

memorize.syntax:
FETCH cursor_name INTO variable;
-- If cursor was never opened, INVALID_CURSOR is raised.
points: INVALID_CURSOR is raised when performing an illegal cursor operation.
Common causes: FETCH on a closed cursor, CLOSE on an unopened cursor.
A cursor must be explicitly OPENed before it can be FETCHed.
Once closed, a cursor cannot be FETCHed until reopened.
Use cursor attributes like %ISOPEN to check the cursor state before operations.

practice.label: CLOSE an unopened cursor
Check cursor state with %ISOPEN

drill: From memory, write a block with an explicit cursor that attempts to FETCH without OPEN and catches INVALID_CURSOR.

title: Handle INVALID_NUMBER

points: INVALID_NUMBER is raised when Oracle cannot convert a string to a number.
TO_NUMBER is the most common trigger, but implicit conversions can also cause it.
Any non-numeric character in the string will cause this exception.
Use functions like REGEXP_LIKE to validate strings before conversion.
This exception can also occur during SQL INSERT/UPDATE with implicit casts.

practice.label: Implicit conversion error
Validate before conversion

drill: From memory, write an anonymous block that attempts to convert a non-numeric string to a number and handles INVALID_NUMBER.

title: Trigger an Exception in a Nested Block and Observe Propagation

memorize.syntax:
BEGIN  -- outer
  BEGIN  -- inner
    -- code that raises an exception
  END;  -- no handler in inner block
  -- this line is skipped
EXCEPTION
  WHEN exception_name THEN
    -- outer block catches propagating exception
END;
points: Unhandled exceptions in an inner block propagate to the outer block.
If the inner block has no handler for the exception, it passes up.
Code after the failing inner block in the outer block is NOT executed.
If the outer block also lacks a handler, the error propagates to the host.
This allows centralized error handling in an outer block.

practice.label: Nested block with propagation to the top

drill: Write from memory a block with a nested inner block. The inner block divides by zero with no handler. The outer block catches ZERO_DIVIDE and prints a message.

title: Handle NO_DATA_FOUND

points: NO_DATA_FOUND is raised when SELECT INTO returns zero rows.
Always wrap SELECT INTO in a block with exception handling.
The EXCEPTION section goes between END; and the final /.
You can handle multiple exception types in the same block.
Without a handler, NO_DATA_FOUND stops the block with an unhandled error.

practice.label: Handle missing department
Handle missing row in a cursor fetch

drill: Write an anonymous block from memory that tries to fetch employee 5000 and handles NO_DATA_FOUND gracefully with a custom message.

title: Use PRAGMA EXCEPTION_INIT

points: PRAGMA EXCEPTION_INIT associates a user-defined exception name with an Oracle error code.
Error code -2291 is the foreign key integrity constraint violation.
This makes exception handlers more readable by giving descriptive names.
You can name any Oracle error, not just the standard predefined ones.
The PRAGMA must be declared in the DECLARE section after the exception declaration.

practice.label: Associate check constraint violation
Name the NOT NULL violation

drill: From memory, write a block that uses PRAGMA EXCEPTION_INIT to name error -2291 and handles it with a descriptive message.

title: Use RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR

memorize.syntax: RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR(-20000, 'Custom error message');
points: RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR raises a user-defined error with a custom message.
Error numbers must be between -20000 and -20999 inclusive.
It appears to the calling environment as an Oracle error with SQLCODE and SQLERRM.
Often used in triggers and procedures to enforce business rules.
RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR causes an immediate exit from the current block.

practice.label: Reject negative values in a calculation
Validate department existence

drill: Write from memory a block that checks a salary and uses RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR with a custom error number and message if the salary is too high.

title: Raise a User-Defined Exception with RAISE

points: RAISE triggers the specified exception immediately.
After RAISE, control transfers to the EXCEPTION section.
You can RAISE any declared exception, predefined or user-defined.
RAISE can be used without arguments inside an EXCEPTION handler to re-raise the current exception.
Always pair RAISE with a corresponding WHEN clause to handle it.

practice.label: Re-raise with RAISE alone

drill: Write from memory a block that declares an exception, raises it inside an IF statement when a value is NULL, and handles it with a message.

title: Handle ROWTYPE_MISMATCH

points: ROWTYPE_MISMATCH is raised when FETCHing into a variable whose type does not match the cursor columns.
This can happen when a record has fewer or more fields than the cursor selects.
Also raised when data types of corresponding fields are incompatible.
Using %ROWTYPE ensures the fetch variable always matches the table structure.
This exception is also raised when assigning a host variable with the wrong type in dynamic SQL.

practice.label: Use %ROWTYPE to avoid mismatch

drill: From memory, write a block that declares a cursor, a mismatched RECORD type, performs a FETCH, and handles ROWTYPE_MISMATCH.

title: Print SQLCODE

points: SQLCODE returns the Oracle error number for the current exception.
ZERO_DIVIDE returns SQLCODE -1476.
NO_DATA_FOUND returns SQLCODE 100 (positive, unlike most).
TOO_MANY_ROWS returns SQLCODE -1422.
In the outermost block WHEN OTHERS, SQLCODE returns the actual error code.
SQLCODE is 0 if no exception is active.

practice.label: Print SQLCODE for NO_DATA_FOUND
Print SQLCODE inside WHEN OTHERS

drill: Write from memory an anonymous block that divides by zero and prints the SQLCODE inside the ZERO_DIVIDE handler.

title: Print SQLERRM

points: SQLERRM returns the full error message associated with the current exception.
For ZERO_DIVIDE, it returns ORA-01476: divisor is equal to zero.
You can pass a negative number to SQLERRM to get a custom message: SQLERRM(-20000).
SQLERRM without arguments returns the message for the current error.
Combine SQLCODE and SQLERRM in WHEN OTHERS for complete error logging.
SQLERRM is particularly useful for debugging complex PL/SQL programs.

practice.label: Print both SQLCODE and SQLERRM
SQLERRM with a custom error number

drill: Write from memory a block that triggers TOO_MANY_ROWS and prints both SQLCODE and SQLERRM in the handler.

title: Handle TOO_MANY_ROWS

points: TOO_MANY_ROWS is raised when SELECT INTO returns more than one row.
SELECT INTO expects exactly one row.
If you expect multiple rows, use an explicit cursor or a cursor FOR loop.
TOO_MANY_ROWS and NO_DATA_FOUND can be handled in the same EXCEPTION section.
This error is common when the WHERE clause is not restrictive enough.

practice.label: Trigger TOO_MANY_ROWS on departments
Handle both NO_DATA_FOUND and TOO_MANY_ROWS

drill: From memory, write a block that queries all employees in department 80 with SELECT INTO and handles TOO_MANY_ROWS with a friendly message.

title: Declare a User-Defined Exception

points: Declare a user-defined exception with: exception_name EXCEPTION;
Place the declaration in the DECLARE section.
Use RAISE exception_name; to trigger it in the executable section.
Handle it in the EXCEPTION section like any predefined exception.
User-defined exceptions do not have an SQLCODE unless linked with PRAGMA EXCEPTION_INIT.

practice.label: Declare and raise custom exception for invalid value
Multiple user-defined exceptions

drill: Write from memory an anonymous block that declares a user-defined exception, checks a condition with IF, raises the exception, and handles it.

title: Use WHEN OTHERS THEN

memorize.syntax:
EXCEPTION
  WHEN named_exception THEN
    -- specific handler
  WHEN OTHERS THEN
    -- catch-all handler
END;
points: WHEN OTHERS catches all exceptions not handled by previous WHEN clauses.
It must always be the LAST exception handler in the EXCEPTION section.
Use it as a safety net to prevent unhandled exceptions from crashing the block.
Inside WHEN OTHERS, you can use SQLCODE and SQLERRM to get error details.
Avoid empty WHEN OTHERS handlers; always log or display the error.

practice.label: Catch-all with multiple specific handlers
WHEN OTHERS with ROLLBACK

drill: Write from memory a block with three exception handlers: NO_DATA_FOUND, ZERO_DIVIDE, and WHEN OTHERS.

title: Handle ZERO_DIVIDE

points: ZERO_DIVIDE is raised when dividing a number by zero.
This applies to both INTEGER and NUMBER types in PL/SQL.
The exception is raised at runtime, not at compilation.
You can prevent the error by checking the divisor before dividing.
ZERO_DIVIDE is a predefined Oracle exception; no declaration needed.

practice.label: Zero divide with employee count
Prevent zero divide with IF

drill: Write from memory an anonymous block that declares two variables, divides by zero, and catches ZERO_DIVIDE with a custom error message.

title: Create Table sadok_departments

points: sadok_departments holds department master data.
dept_id is the PRIMARY KEY, automatically unique and not null.
Use CASCADE CONSTRAINTS when dropping to remove dependent references.
Always COMMIT after DML in user schema to persist changes.
This is the first table of the mini project; sadok_employees will reference it.

practice.label: Add more departments

drill: Write from memory the complete CREATE TABLE statement for sadok_departments with its two columns and primary key.

title: Create Table sadok_employees

memorize.syntax:
CREATE TABLE sadok_employees (
  emp_id   NUMBER PRIMARY KEY,
  emp_name VARCHAR2(50) NOT NULL,
  salary   NUMBER CHECK (salary >= 0),
  dept_id  NUMBER,
  CONSTRAINT fk_dept FOREIGN KEY (dept_id)
    REFERENCES sadok_departments(dept_id)
);
points: emp_id is the PRIMARY KEY of sadok_employees.
salary has a CHECK constraint to ensure it is never negative.
dept_id is a FOREIGN KEY referencing sadok_departments(dept_id).
CASCADE CONSTRAINTS drops the table even if foreign keys reference it.
A trigger will later enforce the non-negative salary check with a custom message.

title: Create Table sadok_log

points: sadok_log is an audit table that records changes to sadok_employees.
log_id uniquely identifies each log entry.
action stores the type of DML operation: INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE.
emp_id references the affected employee.
log_date captures the timestamp of the operation.
This table will be populated by a trigger on sadok_employees.

practice.label: Insert a manual log entry

drill: Write from memory the CREATE TABLE for sadok_log with all four columns and the primary key.

title: Write a Cursor That Displays Employees by Department

points: Use an explicit cursor with a JOIN to combine related tables.
Always declare the fetch variable with cursor%ROWTYPE for type safety.
The standard cursor pattern is: OPEN, LOOP, FETCH, EXIT WHEN, CLOSE.
%NOTFOUND is TRUE after the last row has been fetched.
%ROWCOUNT returns the number of rows fetched so far.

practice.label: Display only Engineering department employees

drill: Write from memory an explicit cursor that joins sadok_employees and sadok_departments, fetches all rows, and displays employee name with department name.

title: Add Exception Handling for Duplicate Employee ID

points: Pre-check with SELECT COUNT(*) before INSERT to avoid duplicates.
The CHECK constraint is validated even if the pre-check passes.
Always include DUP_VAL_ON_INDEX as a safety net.
ROLLBACK in the exception handler to undo the failed INSERT.
Two layers of defense: pre-validation + exception handler.

practice.label: Test duplicate handling with a procedure

drill: Write from memory a block that checks for an existing employee ID before inserting, and handles DUP_VAL_ON_INDEX as a fallback.

title: Add Exception Handling for Employee Not Found

points: Always wrap SELECT INTO in blocks that may query non-existent records.
When NO_DATA_FOUND occurs, provide helpful context like listing valid IDs.
Use FOR loops with implicit cursors to show available alternatives.
This pattern protects users from cryptic Oracle error messages.
Combine with WHEN OTHERS for unexpected errors beyond missing data.
The EXCEPTION block can contain complex recovery logic, not just messages.

practice.label: Employee lookup function with detailed error

drill: Write from memory a block that queries an employee by ID, handles NO_DATA_FOUND with a helpful message listing all valid employee IDs.

title: Create Function get_department_name

points: Functions returning VARCHAR2 need a length-compatible return type.
You can return a default string when the lookup fails.
Use %TYPE to anchor the variable to the table column.
Functions that return VARCHAR2 can be used directly in concatenation.
The return type in the signature must match the RETURN statement value.

practice.label: Use get_department_name in a query

drill: Write from memory a function get_department_name that returns the department name for a given ID, or 'Unknown Department' if not found.

title: Create Function get_employee_salary

points: Functions must have a RETURN clause specifying the return data type.
The RETURN statement sends a value back to the caller.
Functions can be called in SQL queries or PL/SQL expressions.
Return NULL on NO_DATA_FOUND so the caller can check for missing data.
A function must have at least one RETURN statement; every path must end with RETURN.
Call functions by assigning their result to a variable: v := func_name(args);

practice.label: Use the function in a SELECT query

drill: Write from memory a function get_employee_salary that accepts an employee ID, queries the salary, returns it, and returns NULL when the employee is not found.

title: Insert Test Data Using Anonymous Blocks

memorize.syntax:
INSERT INTO sadok_employees (emp_id, emp_name, salary, dept_id)
VALUES (1, 'Alice', 5000, 10);
points: Use anonymous blocks with exception handling to safely insert test data.
Check for existing records with SELECT COUNT(*) to avoid duplicates.
Always COMMIT after successful inserts or ROLLBACK on error.
Test data should cover multiple departments for meaningful queries.
Exception handling prevents the entire batch from failing on a single duplicate.

practice.label: Insert more employees with a loop
