title: Write a Parameterized Cursor That Displays Employees Above a Salary Limit

points: Parameterized cursors accept values at OPEN time, not declaration time.
Parameters make cursors reusable with different filtering criteria.
Specify parameter type (e.g., NUMBER) but NOT size in the declaration.
You can have multiple parameters separated by commas.
The cursor can be opened multiple times with different parameter values.

practice.label: Cursor with two parameters (salary and department)

drill: Write from memory a parameterized cursor that accepts a minimum salary and displays employees earning above that threshold.

title: Create Procedure add_employee

memorize.syntax:
CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE proc_name (
  p_param IN datatype,
  ...
) IS
BEGIN
  -- body
EXCEPTION
  WHEN ... THEN ...
END proc_name;
points: Procedures encapsulate reusable DML operations with parameters.
Use OR REPLACE to modify an existing procedure without dropping it first.
IN parameters receive values from the caller.
Always handle DUP_VAL_ON_INDEX when inserting with user-provided IDs.
Include exception handling inside the procedure for self-contained error management.
COMMIT inside the procedure to finalize the transaction, or let the caller control it.

practice.label: Call add_employee from an anonymous block

drill: Write from memory a procedure add_employee that accepts four IN parameters, inserts into sadok_employees, and handles DUP_VAL_ON_INDEX.

title: Create Procedure increase_salary

points: SELECT ... INTO before UPDATE allows you to reference the old value.
Calculate the new value in PL/SQL rather than in the SQL UPDATE clause.
Handle NO_DATA_FOUND to report missing records gracefully.
Use %TYPE to declare variables that match table column types.
ROLLBACK on error to ensure data consistency.

practice.label: Increase all salaries in a department

drill: Write from memory a procedure increase_salary that accepts an employee ID and a percentage, fetches the current salary, updates it, and handles NO_DATA_FOUND.

title: Create a Trigger That Logs Inserts, Updates, and Deletes on Employees

memorize.syntax:
CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER trigger_name
AFTER INSERT OR UPDATE OR DELETE ON table_name
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
  IF INSERTING THEN
    -- log insert with :NEW values
  ELSIF UPDATING THEN
    -- log update
  ELSIF DELETING THEN
    -- log delete with :OLD values
  END IF;
END;
points: AFTER triggers fire after the DML operation completes.
INSERTING, UPDATING, and DELETING are Boolean predicates for trigger context.
:NEW refers to the row after the change; :OLD refers to the row before.
FOR EACH ROW makes it a row trigger, firing once per affected row.
SELECT NVL(MAX(log_id),0)+1 generates a sequential log_id.
The trigger automatically fires on every qualifying DML; no explicit call needed.

practice.label: Test the trigger with an INSERT

drill: Write from memory a row trigger on sadok_employees that logs every INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE into sadok_log with the action type, employee ID, and current date.

title: Create a Trigger That Rejects Negative Salary Using RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR

points: BEFORE triggers fire before the DML operation; they can reject changes.
RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR inside a trigger aborts the DML operation.
:NEW refers to the new value being inserted or updated.
Use -20000 to -20999 for custom error numbers.
This is the standard way to enforce complex business rules beyond CHECK constraints.
The error message is returned to the caller and can be caught with WHEN OTHERS.

practice.label: Try updating salary to a negative value

drill: Write from memory a BEFORE INSERT OR UPDATE trigger on sadok_employees that rejects negative salaries using RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR.

title: I Can Write an Anonymous PL/SQL Block from Memory

memorize.syntax:
DECLARE
  -- variables, constants, cursors, exceptions
BEGIN
  -- executable code
EXCEPTION
  -- error handlers (optional)
END;
/
points: The anonymous block is the fundamental PL/SQL unit: DECLARE-BEGIN-EXCEPTION-END.
DECLARE is optional if no variables are needed.
EXCEPTION is optional; a block can just be DECLARE-BEGIN-END.
The / character executes the block in SQL*Plus.
SET SERVEROUTPUT ON must be run first to see DBMS_OUTPUT results.

practice.label: Minimal anonymous block

drill: Write from memory a complete anonymous PL/SQL block with DECLARE, BEGIN, EXCEPTION (handle OTHERS), and END. Include at least one variable declaration and one DBMS_OUTPUT line.

title: I Can Use IF, CASE, WHILE, LOOP EXIT WHEN, and FOR

points: IF/ELSIF/ELSE must end with END IF; (note the space).
CASE expression returns a value (can assign); CASE statement uses END CASE;.
WHILE checks condition before each iteration; may execute zero times.
LOOP EXIT WHEN is an unconditional loop with a conditional exit.
FOR loops automatically increment the loop variable; you do not declare it.
FOR i IN REVERSE upper..lower LOOP counts downward.

practice.label: Practice all control structures on HR data

drill: Write from memory a PL/SQL block that uses all five control structures: IF, CASE expression, WHILE (2 iterations), LOOP EXIT WHEN, and FOR (1..3). Each should print its result.

title: I Can Use Cursor Attributes Correctly

memorize.syntax:
cursor%ISOPEN    -- TRUE if cursor is open
cursor%FOUND     -- TRUE if last FETCH returned a row
cursor%NOTFOUND  -- TRUE if last FETCH did not return a row
cursor%ROWCOUNT  -- number of rows fetched so far
points: Before the first FETCH, %FOUND and %NOTFOUND are both NULL.
After the last row, %NOTFOUND is TRUE and %FOUND is FALSE.
%ROWCOUNT is 0 before the first FETCH; increments with each successful FETCH.
%ISOPEN is FALSE before OPEN and after CLOSE; TRUE between OPEN and CLOSE.
Always check %ISOPEN before CLOSE to avoid INVALID_CURSOR.
These attributes apply to explicit cursors only, not implicit cursor FOR loops.

practice.label: Use %NOTFOUND in a WHILE loop

drill: Write from memory a block that opens a cursor, demonstrates the value of all four cursor attributes (%ISOPEN, %FOUND, %NOTFOUND, %ROWCOUNT) before and after the fetch loop, and closes safely.

title: I Can Write Cursor FOR Loops

memorize.syntax:
-- Named cursor FOR loop
FOR record_variable IN cursor_name LOOP
  -- access fields: record_variable.column_name
END LOOP;

-- Inline cursor FOR loop
FOR record_variable IN (SELECT ... FROM ...) LOOP
  -- access fields: record_variable.column_name
END LOOP;
points: Cursor FOR loops automatically OPEN, FETCH, and CLOSE the cursor.
No need to declare the loop variable; it is implicitly %ROWTYPE.
No need to explicitly OPEN, FETCH, EXIT WHEN, or CLOSE.
Inline cursors define the SELECT directly in the FOR statement.
You cannot reference cursor attributes (%FOUND, etc.) inside a cursor FOR loop.
The loop variable is read-only; you cannot assign values to its fields.

practice.label: FOR loop with calculated field

drill: Write from memory two cursor FOR loops: one using a named cursor declared in DECLARE, and one using an inline SQL query directly in the FOR statement. Both should process at least two columns.

title: I Can Write a Full Explicit Cursor from Memory

points: Explicit cursor steps: DECLARE, OPEN, FETCH (in loop), EXIT WHEN, CLOSE.
The cursor query is defined in DECLARE but executed at OPEN.
FETCH retrieves one row at a time into variables or a %ROWTYPE record.
%NOTFOUND becomes TRUE after the last successful fetch.
%ROWCOUNT increments with each successful FETCH.
%ISOPEN checks cursor state; useful in exception handlers to safely CLOSE.
Always CLOSE the cursor, including in exception handlers via %ISOPEN check.

practice.label: Explicit cursor on HR employees by department

drill: Write from memory a complete explicit cursor that selects employees with salary > 8000, fetch-loops through all rows, displays each, reports the row count, and safely closes the cursor even on error.

title: I Can Write Local Procedures and Functions

points: Local procedures and functions are defined in the DECLARE section of any block.
They can only be called from within the block where they are declared.
No CREATE OR REPLACE needed; just PROCEDURE name IS or FUNCTION name RETURN ... IS.
They have full access to all variables declared in the enclosing block.
Use them to organize code within a single anonymous block.
Local subprograms must be declared after variable declarations and before BEGIN.

practice.label: Local function with local variable access

drill: Write from memory an anonymous block with one local procedure (prints a formatted header) and one local function (returns a calculated value). Call both from the main block.

title: I Can Use :NEW and :OLD Correctly

memorize.syntax:
-- In a row trigger:
INSERT: :NEW has values, :OLD is NULL
UPDATE: :NEW has new values, :OLD has old values
DELETE: :OLD has values, :NEW is NULL
points: :NEW is the proposed row value (INSERT/UPDATE); NULL during DELETE.
:OLD is the original row value (UPDATE/DELETE); NULL during INSERT.
In BEFORE triggers, you can modify :NEW column values.
:NEW and :OLD behave like records of the table type.
Prefix with colon: :NEW.column_name, :OLD.column_name.
These are only available in FOR EACH ROW triggers.

practice.label: Prevent decreasing salary on update
Test decrease rejection

drill: Write from memory a row trigger that demonstrates :NEW and :OLD for all three DML operations (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE), printing appropriate messages with column values for each case.

title: I Can Write Parameterized Cursors

points: Parameterized cursors accept values when opened (or in the FOR loop).
Parameter types are declared without size (e.g., NUMBER, not NUMBER(10)).
The same cursor can be used multiple times with different parameter values.
In a cursor FOR loop, parameters are passed in the cursor reference: c_name(val1, val2).
Parameters make cursors flexible and reusable across different filtering scenarios.

practice.label: Parameterized cursor with explicit OPEN/CLOSE

drill: Write from memory a parameterized cursor that accepts a department ID and a minimum salary, then use it in a cursor FOR loop with two different parameter sets.

title: I Can Handle Predefined Exceptions

memorize.syntax:
EXCEPTION
  WHEN NO_DATA_FOUND THEN ...
  WHEN TOO_MANY_ROWS THEN ...
  WHEN ZERO_DIVIDE THEN ...
  WHEN DUP_VAL_ON_INDEX THEN ...
  WHEN INVALID_NUMBER THEN ...
  WHEN INVALID_CURSOR THEN ...
  WHEN CURSOR_ALREADY_OPEN THEN ...
  WHEN OTHERS THEN ...
points: NO_DATA_FOUND: SELECT INTO returned zero rows (SQLCODE 100).
TOO_MANY_ROWS: SELECT INTO returned multiple rows (SQLCODE -1422).
ZERO_DIVIDE: Division by zero (SQLCODE -1476).
DUP_VAL_ON_INDEX: Duplicate value on unique constraint (SQLCODE -1).
INVALID_NUMBER: String-to-number conversion failure (SQLCODE -1722).
INVALID_CURSOR: Illegal cursor operation (SQLCODE -1001).
CURSOR_ALREADY_OPEN: Opening an already open cursor (SQLCODE -6511).
Use nested blocks to isolate risky operations and handle each independently.

practice.label: Handle all predefined exceptions in one block

drill: Write from memory a block that triggers and handles all of: NO_DATA_FOUND, TOO_MANY_ROWS, ZERO_DIVIDE, INVALID_NUMBER, and INVALID_CURSOR. Each test should be in its own nested block.

title: I Can Create Row Triggers

points: Row triggers fire ONCE FOR EACH ROW affected by the DML.
FOR EACH ROW makes it a row trigger.
:NEW refers to the new row value in INSERT/UPDATE operations.
:OLD refers to the old row value in UPDATE/DELETE operations.
In BEFORE triggers, you can modify :NEW values before they are written.
Row triggers can access both :NEW and :OLD to compare old and new values.

Test the audit

title: I Can Use SELECT ... INTO Correctly

memorize.syntax:
SELECT column1, column2, ...
INTO variable1, variable2, ...
FROM table
WHERE condition;
points: SELECT INTO must return exactly one row; zero or multiple rows cause exceptions.
The number and types of INTO variables must match the SELECT list.
Order matters: first column goes to first variable, second to second, etc.
Can use JOINs, aggregations (with GROUP BY ensuring one row), and subqueries.
Always wrap SELECT INTO with NO_DATA_FOUND and TOO_MANY_ROWS handlers.
Use %TYPE for variables to ensure type compatibility with source columns.

drill: Write from memory a block that uses SELECT INTO with a two-table JOIN, fetches three columns into three variables, and handles both NO_DATA_FOUND and TOO_MANY_ROWS.

title: I Can Use SQLCODE, SQLERRM, and RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR

points: SQLCODE returns the Oracle error number (0 if no exception).
SQLERRM returns the full error message including ORA- prefix.
SQLERRM(error_code) returns the message for a specific error code.
RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR(error_number, message) where error_number is -20000 to -20999.
SQLCODE is negative for most errors; NO_DATA_FOUND returns +100 (exception).
Use SQLCODE and SQLERRM in WHEN OTHERS for complete error diagnostics.
RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR immediately exits the block and returns the error to the caller.

title: I Can Create and Call Stored Functions

points: Functions must have a RETURN clause specifying the return data type (no size).
Every execution path must end with a RETURN statement.
Parameters can have DEFAULT values, making them optional for callers.
Call in PL/SQL: v := func_name(args); Call in SQL: SELECT func_name(id) FROM dual;
IN parameters are the default and most common; OUT/IN OUT are possible but unusual in functions.
Exception handlers must also include a RETURN statement.

title: I Can Create and Call Stored Procedures

points: Stored procedures persist in the database schema and can be called from any session.
Parameters can be IN (default), OUT, or IN OUT.
Use OR REPLACE to modify without first dropping.
The procedure name after END is optional but improves readability.
COMMIT inside or let the caller control the transaction.
Call from an anonymous block: proc_name(args); or EXEC proc_name(args); in SQL*Plus.

practice.label: Procedure with OUT parameter

drill: Write from memory a stored procedure that accepts an employee ID, queries their name and salary, displays a greeting, handles NO_DATA_FOUND, and is called from an anonymous block.

title: I Can Create Table Triggers

memorize.syntax:
CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER trigger_name
BEFORE INSERT OR UPDATE OR DELETE ON table_name
-- No FOR EACH ROW = statement-level trigger
BEGIN
  -- fires once per DML statement
END trigger_name;
points: Table triggers (also called statement-level triggers) fire ONCE per DML statement.
They do NOT use FOR EACH ROW.
They fire even if no rows are affected by the DML.
:NEW and :OLD are NOT available in table triggers.
INSERTING, UPDATING, DELETING predicates can be used to branch logic.
Use table triggers for global logging or DML time restrictions.

practice.label: Table trigger that restricts DML to business hours
Test table trigger restriction

drill: Write from memory a table-level trigger on employees that prints a message indicating the DML operation type (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE) and fires before the statement.

title: I Can Create and Raise User-Defined Exceptions

points: User-defined exceptions are declared with: exception_name EXCEPTION;
Use RAISE exception_name; to trigger them explicitly.
PRAGMA EXCEPTION_INIT links a name to an Oracle error code for readable handlers.
RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR creates custom errors visible to the calling environment.
You can mix user-defined, pragma-linked, and predefined exceptions in the same EXCEPTION section.
Re-raise the current exception with RAISE; (no argument) inside a handler.

practice.label: Create and use a custom exception with PRAGMA

drill: Write from memory a block with two user-defined exceptions, one PRAGMA EXCEPTION_INIT, one RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR, and a WHEN OTHERS handler. Each path should be exercised.

title: I Can Use Variables, Constants, %TYPE, %ROWTYPE, and RECORD

points: Variables must be declared in DECLARE before use.
%TYPE anchors a variable to a table column, automatically updating if the table changes.
CONSTANT variables cannot be reassigned after declaration.
%ROWTYPE creates a record variable matching all columns of a table.
Custom RECORD types define structured variables with named fields.
Use := for assignment at declaration time; use := or SELECT INTO for runtime assignment.

practice.label: Declare and use all variable types

drill: Write from memory a block that declares one %TYPE variable, one CONSTANT, one %ROWTYPE, and one custom RECORD type with two fields. Initialize and display all of them.
